title: Start
heading: Start and workflow
content:
This program loads, folds, simulates and fits 57Fe Mössbauer spectra.

Recommended workflow:

  1. Load a local file (.ws5, .adt) or download a measurement from the laboratory web database.
  2. Check the folding point and Vmax. If a NORMOS .RES/.PLT sidecar exists, initial values are imported automatically.
  3. Adjust the baseline and verify the normalization.
  4. Choose a model: discrete singlet/doublet/sextet or a distribution P(BHF)/P(ΔEQ).
  5. Fix known parameters and release only the parameters justified by the data.
  6. Run the fit and inspect residuals, areas and correlations.
  7. Save the fit, the full JSON session and/or a Markdown/PDF report.

Notation:

  δ     isomer shift, in mm/s, relative to room-temperature α-Fe.
  ΔEQ   quadrupole splitting, in mm/s.
  BHF   magnetic hyperfine field, in T.
  Γ     line half-width at half maximum, HWHM, in mm/s.

title: Mössbauer basics
heading: Physical background
content:
57Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy measures recoil-free resonant absorption of the 14.4 keV gamma ray. The Doppler velocity of the source scans the resonance energy.

Main hyperfine interactions:

  • Electric monopole interaction: isomer shift δ. It reflects electron density at the nucleus and is sensitive to oxidation state and bonding.
  • Electric quadrupole interaction: quadrupole splitting ΔEQ. It reflects the electric field gradient at the nucleus.
  • Magnetic dipole interaction: hyperfine field BHF. It produces the six-line magnetic sextet.

For a randomly oriented powder without texture, ideal sextet intensities follow the ratio:

  lines 1,6 : lines 2,5 : lines 3,4 = 3 : 2 : 1

The relative intensity parameters can be adjusted for textured samples or external-field measurements.

title: Files and web
heading: Data loading and web access
content:
Supported input files:

  • Modern WS5 files, XML files with a <data> block.
  • Older ADT files, plain count lists without XML header.
  • NORMOS sidecar files (.RES, .PLT, .JOB) are partially read when present.

Main file actions:

  Load...
    Opens local spectra. If a NORMOS .RES file is found next to the spectrum, the final folding point and starting parameters are imported when possible.

  Web measurements...
    Lists and downloads measurements through the laboratory REST API. Credentials are used to obtain a token; the password is not stored.

  Web calibrations...
    Lists and downloads α-Fe calibrations. If a measurement has an associated calibration, the calibrated Vmax can be applied.

  Use current file as calibration...
    Marks the currently loaded spectrum as a local calibration, without needing to
    download it from the web. Useful when you open a .ws5 or .adt file that contains
    an α-Fe or other velocity standard pattern.
    The dialog asks for:
      • Sample: name of the standard (pre-filled with the file name).
      • Vmax (mm/s): if provided, applied immediately to the active spectrum.
      • IS (mm/s): isomer shift of the standard (informational only).
    On confirmation the program records the calibration with source "local" (as opposed
    to "web_api" for web-sourced calibrations). The information is saved in the JSON
    session and appears in the info panel and exported report.

  Save fit...
    Exports a text table with velocity, normalized data, model, residual and folded counts. In distribution mode it also exports P(BHF) or P(ΔEQ).

  Save session...
    Stores the complete state in JSON: data, parameters, fixed/free flags, components, covariance, errors, constraints and calibration traceability.

  Export Markdown/PDF report...
    Creates a human-readable report with parameters, statistics, residual diagnostics, areas, correlations and calibration information. The Markdown file is always kept, even if a PDF is also generated.

title: Folding
heading: Folding, velocity axis and background
content:
The folding point is the internal symmetry center used to fold the two halves of the spectrum. Fractional values are supported, as in NORMOS.

Important parameters:

  Vmax
    Maximum velocity of the transducer, used to build the -Vmax ... +Vmax velocity axis. It should normally come from an α-Fe calibration. Signed Vmax values are allowed: a negative Vmax is kept negative and reverses the velocity-axis direction, matching web calibrations or NORMOS files that carry a signed velocity.

  Signed velocities and edge trimming
    The modular GUI discards the first and last points of the folded spectrum because these edge channels are often less reliable. After removing those two points the program does not rebuild a shorter axis from -Vmax to +Vmax, because that would stretch the velocity scale and bias BHF. Instead it builds the full NORMOS-like axis first and trims the same positions in both data and velocity:

      full_velocity = linspace(-Vmax, +Vmax, N)
      used_data     = full_data[1:-1]
      used_velocity = full_velocity[1:-1]

    If Vmax is fitted, this already-trimmed axis is scaled; it is not rebuilt with false end points.

  Fit Vmax
    Includes Vmax as a free parameter. This can compensate small calibration errors, but BHF and Vmax are strongly correlated, so BHF should normally be fixed when Vmax is fitted.

  Folding point
    Internal channel center. The GUI also displays an approximate NORMOS folding value.

  Fit folding point
    Includes the folding center as a free parameter in discrete fits. At each iteration the counts are refolded and statistical weights are recomputed. Use it only near a reasonable starting value and with a limited number of free parameters.

  Baseline and slope
    Model the normalized transmission background. Do not use a large slope to hide a wrong folding point.

A wrong folding point often produces antisymmetric positive-negative residual pairs around the absorption lines.

title: Discrete model
heading: Singlets, doublets and sextets
content:
Each component tab can be configured as a singlet, doublet or sextet.

  Singlet
    One absorption line. Main parameters: δ, Γ, depth and intensity.

  Doublet
    Two lines separated by ΔEQ. Useful for paramagnetic Fe sites with quadrupole interaction.

  Sextet
    Six magnetic lines. Main parameters: δ, ΔEQ, BHF, Γ, depth and relative intensities. The default starting values are depth = 0.02 for component 1 and Γ = 0.15 mm/s. The depth slider is limited to 0–0.07 for easier manual modelling, but the internal fit bound remains wider.

  NORMOS-style intensities
    int3 is hidden and internally fixed to 1: lines 3&4 are the implicit reference. The visible controls are int1≈D13 and int2≈D23. For a random powder use int1=3, int2=2, int3=1; depth/DEP is the global absorption scale.

Line profile (Options ▸ Line profile):

  Lorentzian
    Default profile. Appropriate for natural or homogeneous broadening.

  Voigt
    Convolution of a Lorentzian (width γ) with a Gaussian of σ = {voigt_sigma:.3g} mm/s. Useful for additional instrumental or static broadening. Areas are computed by numerical integration, so Lorentzian and Voigt profiles are treated consistently.

  σ is the global Gaussian width and only applies to the Voigt profile. The Fit σ checkbox next to the σ slider in Velocity, folding and background lets σ be a free parameter of the fit alongside vmax and the folding point; it is active only in Voigt mode and is unchecked by default. Right-click the σ slider/box to switch quickly between Lorentzian and Voigt. Mind the σ–γ correlation, which can inflate the error bars if the data do not constrain the line shape well.

The Fit button optimizes all non-fixed parameters. The status panel reports RMS, χ²red, AIC, BIC, areas, errors and high correlations when available.

title: P(BHF)
heading: Hyperfine field distribution P(BHF)
content:
Distribution mode represents the spectrum as a sum of many sextets with different hyperfine fields. The result P(BHF) is the spectral weight associated with each BHF value.

Method:

  • Define a BHF grid between B min and B max.
  • Each grid point contributes a sextet.
  • Weights are constrained to be non-negative.
  • The second difference of P(BHF) is penalized to avoid non-physical oscillations.

The minimized expression is schematically:

  weighted spectral residual² + α · roughness(P)²

The residual is weighted using Poisson uncertainties estimated from the folded counts. Therefore channels with higher statistical noise contribute less to the cost.

title: P(ΔEQ)
heading: Quadrupole-splitting distribution P(ΔEQ)
content:
The distribution variable can be switched from BHF to ΔEQ in the Distribution tab. In that case the grid limits are expressed in mm/s and the model represents a distribution of quadrupole splittings.

Typical uses:

  • Disordered paramagnetic Fe environments.
  • Ferritin-like or amorphous phases.
  • Samples where a single doublet is too simple but a magnetic distribution is not appropriate.

The controls for α, bins, Γ, δ and line shape work in the same way as for P(BHF).

title: L-curve
heading: Choosing the regularization parameter α
content:
The L-curve button scans a range of α values and opens a window with:

  • log residual norm versus log roughness.
  • RMS and reduced χ² versus α.
  • the maximum-curvature L-curve suggestion.
  • an additional compromise suggestion.
  • a button to save the complete scan table.

Small α values may overfit noise and create spurious peaks. Large α values oversmooth the distribution and may hide real structure. The L-curve is a guide, not an automatic proof.

title: Constraints
heading: Linear constraints and physical presets
content:
Linear constraints impose:

  target = factor · source + offset

The target parameter is removed from the optimizer and updated from the source at each model evaluation.

Typical uses:

  • Equal line widths between components.
  • Equal isomer shifts.
  • Fixed intensity ratios.
  • Reduced number of free parameters when correlations are high.

Physical presets:

  Options → Physical constraint presets...

They can quickly impose powder sextet intensities 3:2:1, equal widths within components, equal δ between active components, or equal Γ1 between active components.

title: Statistics
heading: Weights, model selection and robust errors
content:
Poisson weights:

  The fit uses uncertainties estimated from folded counts. The minimized residual is (model - data) / σ.

Reduced χ², AIC and BIC:

  Reduced χ² measures weighted residuals per degree of freedom. AIC and BIC penalize the number of free parameters and help compare models fitted to the same data.

Numerical areas:

  Areas are obtained by integrating the actual component profile on the velocity axis. This is valid for both Lorentzian and Voigt profiles.

Correlations:

  High |r| values in the covariance matrix mean that two parameters are not independently determined. Fixing one parameter, adding a physically justified constraint or simplifying the model may be better than adding more freedom.

Bootstrap Monte Carlo:

  Fit → Bootstrap errors (MC)... generates synthetic spectra by adding noise to the fitted model and refits each replica. The standard deviation of fitted parameters gives a more robust uncertainty estimate for discrete models.

title: Diagnostics
heading: Residuals and common problems
content:
The residual, data minus model, should look like random statistical noise.

Common patterns:

  • Antisymmetric positive-negative pairs around lines: wrong folding point.
  • Curved or sloped background: wrong Vmax, normalization problem or real detector trend.
  • Model lines broader than data: Γ too large.
  • Residual peaks in the line wings: consider Voigt profile or a distribution.
  • Structure in distribution mode: α may be too large or the model may be incomplete.
  • High-frequency oscillations in P(BHF): α is probably too small.

The status panel reports lag-1 autocorrelation, a runs-test z value and an antisymmetric residual indicator.

title: Saving and reports
heading: Saving results, sessions and reports
content:
Save fit (.dat):

  Exports velocity, normalized data, model, residual and folded counts. Distribution fits include the distribution table.

Save session (.json):

  Saves the full working state, including parameters, fixed/free flags, components, covariance, errors, constraints and status text. If calibration metadata are available, they are stored too.

Export Markdown/PDF report:

  Creates a documented report with version, file, folding point, Vmax, calibration, metrics, areas, parameters, errors, correlations and residual diagnostics. If PDF export is selected, the Markdown file is still preserved.

Automatic settings are stored in:

  {settings_path_str}

title: Reference values
heading: Typical 57Fe reference parameters
content:
Room-temperature values relative to α-Fe, approximate only:

  α-Fe:          δ ≈ 0.00 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 0.00 mm/s, BHF ≈ 33.0 T.
  Fe3+ oct.:     δ ≈ 0.30-0.45 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 0.4-1.0 mm/s.
  Fe2+ oct.:     δ ≈ 1.0-1.3 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 2.0-3.5 mm/s.
  Magnetite A:   δ ≈ 0.28 mm/s, BHF ≈ 49 T.
  Magnetite B:   δ ≈ 0.65 mm/s, BHF ≈ 46 T.
  Hematite:      δ ≈ 0.37 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ -0.2 mm/s, BHF ≈ 52 T.
  Goethite:      δ ≈ 0.37 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ -0.25 mm/s, BHF ≈ 38 T.
  Ferritin:      δ ≈ 0.45-0.50 mm/s, ΔEQ ≈ 0.7-1.0 mm/s.

Temperature, substitutions, particle size and disorder may shift these values.

title: Accessing options & new features
heading: Recent options and how to reach them
content:
This chapter summarizes recently added features and, above all, WHERE they are enabled in the interface.

1. "Fit → Advanced fit options" menu

  Groups the optimizer's fine settings:

  • Line profile: Lorentzian or Voigt (the Voigt is normalized to its peak analytically, independent of sampling). σ (global Gaussian width) is only active with the Voigt profile; use the Fit σ checkbox next to the σ slider to fit σ as a free parameter.
  • Likelihood: Gauss (χ², data σ; default) or Poisson (IRLS, model σ). Poisson rescales the uncertainty from the model at each iteration.
  • Robust loss: controls how the optimizer treats large residuals (isolated bad channels, spurious spikes, folding defects or poorly normalized points).
      - Linear: classical least squares. Recommended for clean spectra and for final comparable results; all channels are weighted according to σ.
      - Soft L1: smooth transition between quadratic and linear behavior. Usually the most conservative robust option when only a few points look suspicious.
      - Huber: quadratic near zero and linear for large residuals. Useful when there are clear outliers.
    Important: it does not fix an incomplete physical model or bad calibration/folding. If enabling a robust loss changes the parameters strongly, inspect the responsible channels and report which loss was used.
  • Propagate calibration σ (vmax): adds the velocity-scale uncertainty in quadrature to the weights; realistically inflates the δ and BHF errors.
  • Global optimization (DE) before TRF: a differential-evolution pre-pass to escape local minima in multi-sextet spectra (slower; optional).
  • P(BHF): add active sharp components / refine global δ and Γ.

2. Context menus (right-click) on the sextet sliders

  The intensity mode and the quadrupole treatment are chosen with the RIGHT mouse button on the relevant sliders:

  • Right-click on I13/I23/I or the t slider → "Intensity mode": Free (independent i1,i2,i3) or Texture (a single parameter t = sin²θ giving 3 : 4t/(2−t) : 1; t=2/3 yields the 3:2:1 random-powder ratio).
  • Right-click on β or ΔEQ → "Quadrupole treatment": 1st order (rigid pattern, historic), Kündig fixed (diagonalizes the Hamiltonian with free β) or Kündig powder (polycrystalline average).

  Parameters not used for the current type or mode are shown greyed out (disabled): e.g. a singlet disables ΔEQ, BHF and the unused widths/intensities; Texture mode disables i1,i2,i3 leaving only t; β is only active with "Kündig fixed".

3. P(BHF) / P(ΔEQ) distribution

  • Regularization: in the distribution panel choose Tikhonov (smooth, second difference) or Total Variation (TV, sharp edges).
  • α selection: the L-curve dialog proposes three values — L-curve corner, compromise and GCV (generalized cross-validation) — with a button to apply each.
  • The 1σ band around P is drawn automatically (clipped to P≥0).
  • Reduced χ² uses effective degrees of freedom, not the raw bin count.

4. Simulation behavior

  • When you load a file NO model is drawn: only the data are shown. The simulation appears as soon as you touch a model parameter (slider/entry), run a fit, or load a session.
  • "Initialize from minima" (Fit menu) detects the subspectra, activates the corresponding components, and scales the depths so the initial model matches the data level.


5. Quick calibration from the sample box

  In the file/sample box, RIGHT-CLICK opens a menu to use the loaded spectrum as calibration:

  • "Use as calibration (current vmax & iso)": sets the calibration taking directly the current velocity (vmax) and isomer shift (δ of the first active sextet), with no dialog.
  • "Use as calibration (details)...": opens the dialog with editable fields (name, vmax, iso).

6. Corrected isomer shift

  If a calibration with a reference iso is active, the program computes the CORRECTED δ of each component:

    δ_corr = δ_measured − iso_calibration

  which shifts the velocity zero to the calibration standard (e.g. α-Fe). It appears both in the results box (a "corrected δ" line per component) and in the Markdown/PDF report (a "Corrected δ" section). Shown only when the calibration carries a reference iso.

title: Command-line fitting
heading: CLI: template + spectrum → result JSON
content:
The mossbauer_fit_cli.py script lets you fit one spectrum outside the GUI from a template file (a saved session .json). Useful to process many heterogeneous files in shell loops, embed fits in a pipeline, or reproduce results without opening the interface.

Basic usage:

  python mossbauer_fit_cli.py \
      --template template.json \
      --spectrum measurement.adt \
      --out result.json

Arguments:

  --template   Template .json file. Either a full session saved from the
               GUI (File ▸ Save session...) or a JSON with just the
               model_state block.
  --spectrum   Spectrum .ws5 or .adt to fit.
  --out        Output .json path.
  --vmax       (optional) Overrides the template's vmax.
  --quiet      Suppress the stdout summary (exit code only).

Exit codes:

  0   fit OK.
  1   error (missing file, fit failure, etc.).

Examples:

  # One compound, hand-made template
  python mossbauer_fit_cli.py --template T.json --spectrum a.adt --out a.fit.json

  # Many different files, each with its own template, from shell
  for f in data/*.adt; do
      python mossbauer_fit_cli.py \
          --template templates/$(basename "$f" .adt).json \
          --spectrum "$f" \
          --out results/$(basename "$f" .adt).fit.json
  done

  # Headless server (no display)
  xvfb-run -a python mossbauer_fit_cli.py \
      --template T.json --spectrum a.adt --out a.fit.json

Output JSON format:

  Same structure as a saved GUI session (counts, model_state, last_fit, calibration, ...) plus a batch_fit_result section with:
    • spectrum, template: paths used.
    • values: final values of all active parameters.
    • errors: 1σ errors per free parameter.
    • stats: chi2, red_chi2, dof, AIC, BIC, residual diagnostics.
    • free_keys: list of fitted parameters.

  Can be reloaded as a session in the GUI or reused as a template for the next fit.

Ready-to-use templates in data_sample/:

  template_alphaFe.json     Metallic α-Fe (sextet, BHF≈33 T)
  template_hematita.json    Hematite α-Fe2O3 (sextet, BHF≈51.5 T, 2ε≈-0.20)
  template_magnetita.json   Magnetite Fe3O4 (two sextets, sites A and B)
  template_siderita.json    Siderite FeCO3 (paramagnetic Fe²⁺ doublet)

Building a template by hand:

  Easiest path: load a representative spectrum in the GUI, fit (or set parameters manually), and do File ▸ Save session... — the resulting .json is a valid template. The files in data_sample/ are minimal editable examples.

  Minimum expected structure (model_state):
    • vars            initial values of all model parameters.
    • fixed           which parameters stay fixed during the fit.
    • sextet_enabled  which components (1, 2, 3, ...) are active.
    • component_kind  type per component: Sextete / Doblete / Singlete.
    • intensity_mode  free or texture, per component.
    • quad_treatment  1st_order, kundig_fixed or kundig_powder, per component.
    • line_profile    Lorentziana or Voigt.
    • likelihood      gauss or poisson.
    • robust_loss     linear, soft_l1 or huber.
    • constraints     list of linear constraints (optional).

  The folding center is NOT taken from the template: each spectrum auto-detects its own. vmax is (calibration); can be overridden with --vmax.

title: What is new
heading: New functions since version 0.2
content:
Recent improvements include:

  • Poisson-weighted fitting for discrete models and distributions.
  • Reduced χ², AIC and BIC.
  • Numerical areas for Lorentzian and Voigt profiles.
  • Parameter correlation summaries.
  • Extended L-curve with reduced χ² and exportable table.
  • Residual diagnostics.
  • Deterministic multi-start fitting.
  • Progress windows for long calculations.
  • Markdown/PDF report export.
  • Bootstrap Monte Carlo errors for discrete fits.
  • Physical constraint presets.
  • Optional folding-point fitting.
  • Calibration uncertainty traceability when available.
v0.4.2 — Local-file calibration:

  New menu item File → "Use current file as calibration...".
  If you load a .ws5 or .adt that turns out to be a calibration spectrum
  (α-Fe or another velocity standard), you can now tell the program without
  having to download it from the web database. The dialog asks for:
    • Sample: standard name (pre-filled with the file name).
    • Vmax (mm/s): if provided, applied immediately to the active spectrum
      (same as a web-sourced calibration).
    • IS (mm/s): isomer shift of the standard (informational only).
  On confirmation the program records the calibration with source="local"
  (as opposed to "web_api" for API-sourced calibrations). The information
  is saved in the JSON session and appears in the info panel and report.
v0.4.3 — Active calibration display:

  A new label in the file panel (just below the spectrum name) always
  shows which calibration is active and its parameters:
    Line 1 — Calibration [local] or [web_api] and sample name.
    Line 2 — Vmax and IS (if available).
  The label hides when a new file is loaded and reappears when a session
  with saved calibration is restored.

  Also, the 'Use current file as calibration...' dialog now pre-fills
  the Vmax field with the current slider value and the IS field with the
  δ of the first active sextet, so you can confirm with one click if the
  fit is already done.
v0.4.4 — Persistent calibration across file loads:

  Bug fix: opening a new .ws5/.adt file no longer clears the active
  calibration. The calibration reflects the instrument state (velocity
  scale), not the data file, and now persists until the user explicitly
  sets a new one via the local calibration dialog or web download.
v0.4.5 — Extended BHF and AEQ ranges:

  • BHF up to 60 T: hyperfine field sliders in discrete sextets,
    fixed BHF in distribution mode and B min of the integration range.
  • Negative AEQ: AEQ (sextets) and fixed ΔEQ (distribution) sliders
    change from [0, 4] to [−4, 4] mm/s, allowing negative electric
    field gradients.
v0.4.6 — Sample data and improved file filter:

  • The data_sample/ folder included in the ZIP contains two ready-to-
    open example spectra: calibration.adt (α-Fe calibration) and
    Fe3O4.adt (magnetite), each with a pre-fitted session file.
    To use them: File → Open... the .adt, then
    File → Load session... the corresponding .json.
  • The open dialog now also accepts *.WS5 and *.ADT (uppercase),
    useful on Windows where file names may be uppercase.
v0.4.7 — Automatic pip on update and startup:

  • After installing an update, the program automatically runs
    pip install -r requirements.txt and shows the result in the
    final message.
  • At startup, if requirements.txt is newer than the last recorded
    check, pip runs in the background without interrupting the user.
    Keeps dependencies up to date without any manual steps.
v0.4.8 — Line profile from the σ slider:

  • Right-click the σ slider to switch between the two line profiles
    (Lorentzian and Voigt) without opening the Options menu.
v0.4.9 — Asymmetric profile-likelihood errors:

  • New Fit → Profile-likelihood errors... For every free parameter of
    the last discrete fit, profiles Δχ²(p) (fixing p, refitting the
    rest) and locates Δχ²=1 (1σ) and Δχ²=4 (2σ) crossings, producing
    asymmetric +Δ⁺/−Δ⁻ bars.
  • Useful when the likelihood is not parabolic, with strong correlations
    (σ–γ, BHF–δ in overlapping sextets), or when the fit hits a physical
    bound. Complementary to the bootstrap MC.
  • Asymmetric bars appear in the Status panel and the Markdown/PDF
    report; on completion a window shows the Δχ²(p) curves with the
    Δχ²=1 and 4 cut lines.
v0.4.10 — Batch fit (sequential warm-start):

  • New Fit → Batch fit (series)... Select N .ws5/.adt files and fit
    them one by one, using the active model as the template and the
    previous fit's result as the starting point of the next one
    (warm-start). Useful for temperature/field series.
  • Optional metadata extraction (T, B...) from the file name using a
    regex with a (?P<v>...) group.
  • Failure-tolerant: if a file fails to load/fit it is marked as
    "failed" and the series continues.
  • Outputs: TSV export with values and errors per spectrum, and a
    trend window plotting each free parameter vs metadata (with error
    bars).

v0.4.11 — NORMOS compatibility and velocity axis:

  • Signed Vmax is accepted and preserved in the GUI, CLI and web calibrations.
  • The depth slider is 0–0.07 for fine manual control, while the internal fit bound stays wider.
  • Defaults: component-1 depth = 0.02 and Γ = 0.15 mm/s.
  • int3 is hidden/fixed to 1; int1≈D13 and int2≈D23 follow the NORMOS convention.
  • Right-click σ to switch Lorentzian/Voigt.
  • When the first and last folded channels are trimmed, the original velocity axis is trimmed too instead of being rebuilt to ±Vmax. This avoids velocity-scale stretching and systematic BHF bias.
